preamble: The transformation of a voiced velar fricative ([ɣ]) into a voiceless velar plosive ([k]) represents a phonetic strengthening process known as fortition. This shift involves both the loss of vocal cord vibration (devoicing) and a change in the manner of articulation from a continuous, turbulent airflow (fricative) to a complete closure followed by a sudden release (plosive). While lenition (weakening) is a more common sound change, fortition of this type is attested in specific linguistic contexts.

examples.note: Shift in the aspect marker for 2nd person plural in verbal inflection.
Shift in the aspect marker for 3rd person plural in verbal inflection.
Intervocalic /ɣ/ shifted to /k/ with concomitant lengthening of the preceding vowel.
Allophonic variation where [ɣ] can be realized as [k].

sources: Nefedov, A. (2015). Clause linkage in Ket. Leiden University.
